data.title: Enbridge: The Indirect AI Winner
data.description:
Investment thesis

My bullish call on Enbridge (NYSE:ENB) back in March has worked out well. The stock has returned around 23% since then, beating the broader U.S. stock market. For an energy infrastructure company with steady cash flows, that’s an impressive performance.

Despite global macroeconomic uncertainty, I remain extremely bullish on ENB. This is a company with rock-solid fundamentals and a unique position in the North American midstream industry. Furthermore, unlike oil producers whose profits are tied to the rise and fall of crude prices, ENB earns the bulk of its revenue from transporting and processing energy. And this is a setup that generates a highly stable cash flow. Another reason for my bullish stance is the current AI revolution. There’s no denying that running data centers sucks up an enormous amount of power, much of which is still sourced from natural-gas-fired plants. As ENB owns and operates a massive natural gas pipeline, it is in the perfect position to benefit from the AI wave. Finally, ENB’s valuation remains highly attractive. Add in the steady cash flow, solid dividends, and AI-driven demand, I believe this company is a compelling investment opportunity. All in all, I am reiterating my "Strong Buy" rating.

Recent developments

ENB’s latest quarterly earnings, released two months ago, were excellent. A quick recap: The company delivered a double beat against Wall Street’s expectations. And these weren’t marginal wins either. There were some quite surprising positives, reflecting a strong operation.

Both revenue and EPS demonstrated solid year-over-year growth, fueled mainly by Gas Transmission, which is the core of ENB’s business model, and this business's growth has bright future as it is closely linked to secular tailwinds that I will discuss below.

Thanks to solid and stable profitability, ENB’s free cash flow remains strong and supports the company’s financial health. The balance sheet is stellar, with a total debt of $74 billion, significantly lower than the $108 billion market cap. While you could argue that the debt looks a little high compared to the sub-$1-billion cash balance as of the latest reportable date, it is actually a fairly standard figure for a capital-intensive business like oil and gas midstream. After all, this is a business that requires heavy upfront investment in infrastructure, an investment that goes on to yield steady cash flows for decades to come. So the fact that total debt is notably lower than the market cap indicates that Enbridge’s financial flexibility for new potential projects is actually quite strong.

The strength of the balance sheet gives ENB the financial flexibility to keep expanding into new projects, while also supporting its dividend. I therefore believe ENB’s 5.44% forward dividend yield is safe. Moreover, during the latest earnings call, the management reaffirmed their 2025 full-year and multi-year outlooks, showing a strong confidence in sustaining and growing payouts.

For some investors, a 5.5% dividend yield might not seem that appealing when U.S. rates are still relatively high. For investors looking for a safer option, I recommend taking a look at the Series 1 Cumulative Preferred Shares (OTC:EBBGF), currently offering a 6.7% forward yield. Dividends of preferred shares have priority over common stock dividends, meaning higher safety. Personally, I would rather stick to the common shares due to my age and risk appetite, but I believe the latter option would be interesting for more risk-averse investors.

The crucial thing to remember here is that ENB is not only distributing dividends, it’s also heavily investing in growth. And AI is where it’s at. I’d like to highlight several new projects and tailwinds that not only show just how aggressively this company is preparing for an AI-driven future, but also what a pivotal role it will play in the AI’s overall rollout.

I’ll start with ENB’s recent announcement to go ahead with two U.S. transmission projects as part of its $23 billion capital program. These expansions are tied to the growing demand from data centers and LNG exports. The Northeast expansions will supply gas to power-thirsty data centers, while the Permian-to-Gulf Coast pipeline will feed the LNG exports that are increasingly fueling the global AI infrastructure. These assets will lock in contracted revenue for decades to come, right at a time when AI energy demand is about to explode.

A major push for ENB is the sheer volume of gas required to run AI infrastructure. According to EQT’s CEO, AI is expected to drive a staggering 20-40% increase in U.S. natural gas demand. This is when ENB’s extensive pipeline network will really come into its own, providing a critical part of the infrastructure required as it transports gas from production areas to the energy-thirsty data centers and LNG export facilities. In short, whether it’s data centers in Virginia or LNG exports in Louisiana. ENB is the crucial middleman.

Citi adds weight to this development by predicting that hyperscale cloud providers like Amazon, Microsoft, and Google will spend $2.8 trillion by 2029 on building AI data centers. That’s a mind-blowing number. As per above, these are projects that demand a vast amount of power, and mainly natural gas. And once again ENB’s network is right in the heart of the supply chain.

Politics may add another tailwind, especially if President Trump’s recent comments are anything to go by. His stance reflects his general skepticism toward renewables and a preference for fossil fuels. In this context, I predict U.S. policy will lean towards supporting such energy sources over wind and solar power, meaning that natural gas pipelines such as those owned by ENB will become even more strategically important.

The above bullish factors highlight one simple point: the AI boom is also an energy transportation boom, where natural gas is the fuel that can make AI happen. And ENB is in the perfect position to capture a large share of the infrastructure market. As a result, investor sentiment is also improving, backed by the share price momentum. While the stock has underperformed in the broader market for much of this year, the momentum has definitely turned.

Finally, seasonality is likely to add an end-of-year boost. As we can see below, ENB historically performs well in the last two months of the year. Taking into account the aforementioned bullish conditions, I don’t believe 2025 will be any different.

Valuation update

If we look at the valuation, ENB actually grades quite low across standard ratios compared to the sector median. However, I don’t find these comparisons particularly meaningful. Here’s why: ENB is unique with an unrivaled and extensive presence across North America. So instead of comparing sector medians, I prefer to study historical averages. And from this perspective, ENB’s valuation looks attractive. The company is not trading significantly above its fundamentals, which is to be expected as it is not a growth stock. It’s an infrastructure utility with a reliable cash flow.

Looking ahead, Wall Street analysts expect the forward P/E ratio to compress, supporting my belief that ENB is attractively valued. In short, investors are paying for a pipeline of contracted projects that will support higher profits in the future.

Risks update

Several risks stand out and are worth mentioning. Firstly, as a large company transporting hydrocarbons, ENB comes under major ongoing environmental, social, and governance scrutiny. New and constantly changing regulations could impose stricter environmental standards, which would mean the company would have to increase spending on factors such as safety and maintenance, incurring financial costs and losing time. A delay in just one pipeline could cost the company millions in revenue.

Another risk is the economy itself. ENB will not be immune to the U.S. economic slowdown. While the company is less sensitive to oil and gas prices than producers, it is still dependent on volumes, and these could weaken significantly in the wake of slowing energy usage in case of the broader macroeconomic cyclical downturn.

Bottom line

To conclude, ENB remains a "Strong Buy". Financially, ENB continues to deliver on all fronts with a stable cash flow, solid balance sheet, and attractive dividends. This is bolstered by both long-term growth opportunities and political tailwinds. With the company’s heavy investment into gas transmission projects, it is not only poised to capitalize on the AI boom but also to become a pivotal part of its infrastructure. The result is an attractive valuation, especially from a historical perspective, with strong dividend safety.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ChargePoint: Potential Value Trap - Structural/Growth/Bottom-Line Headwinds Remain
data.description:
CHPT's Headwinds Mount As The Stock Enters Oversold Territories

I previously covered ChargePoint Holdings (NYSE:CHPT) in April 2024, discussing how the stock might remain volatile, attributed to the dangerous combination of being highly shorted and the penny stock prices.

Despite the growing annualized subscription revenues and the growing stickiness of its platform, the company had remained unprofitable then, worsened by the plateauing first round of EV adoption, the record high borrowing costs, and the delayed launch of affordable mass-market EVs.

CHPT 1Y Stock Price

Since that reiterated Hold rating, CHPT has indeed retraced by another -59.5% compared to the wider market at +33.4%, while it notably underperforms its EV charging peers by large degrees.

Much of its headwinds are attributed to the new US administration's reversal in EV policies, as observed in the One Big Beautiful Bill halting these tax credits:

  • the 30D New Clean Vehicle Credit ($7.5K EV tax credit for consumers) from September 30, 2025 onwards,
  • the 30C Alternative Fuel Vehicle Refueling Property Credit (electric vehicle charging equipment) from June 30, 2026 onwards, and
  • the 45W Commercial Clean Vehicle Credit ($40K EV tax credit for fleet operators) from September 30, 2025 onwards,

with it apparent that the EV sector is likely to face a drastically different supply and demand pattern, assuming that spending trends and investment cycles are impacted by the changing regulatory environment.

This is especially since CHPT has been reporting a painfully decelerating top-line growth profile after the explosive double-digit growth observed in FY2022 and FY2023, worsened by its inherent lack of profitability thus far.

The management has further fed the pessimism with a mixed FQ3'26 revenue guidance of $95M at the midpoint (-4% QoQ/-12.4% YoY) and a pushed out EBITDA breakeven target beyond FY2026, with it hinting at further growth headwinds and elevated cash burn cadence ahead.

With a deteriorating balance sheet at a growing net debt position of -$115.29M in FQ2'26 (-3% QoQ/-171.8% YoY) and a negative free cash flow generation of -$41.47M in H1'26 (+65.7% YoY), I believe that CHPT may further dilute its existing shareholders moving forward.

This is based on the last share count of 23.19M (+8.5% YoY), the September 2025 $400M mixed shelf offering, and the elevated annualized stock based compensation of $72.14M in H1'26 (-10.6% YoY), despite the recently completed reverse stock split at a ratio of 1-for-20 in July 2025.

This is especially since CHPT continues to rely on Networked Charging Systems sales to drive its top-line growth, with the ChargePoint as a Service subscription revenues remaining a small portion of its overall FQ2'26 revenues at $39.89M (+4.9% QoQ/+10.2% YoY) at a ratio of 40.4% (+1.4 points QoQ/+7.1 YoY).

Pending the recovery of the North American region attributed to the mixed regulatory policy, there appear to be minimal growth prospects in Europe as well, as observed in the latter's YoY revenue decline in FQ2'26.

At a time when its direct competitor, EVgo (EVGO), is consistently reporting a double-digit growth profile, while raising their FY2025 guidance, it is unsurprising that CHPT's underperformance has been translated to the stock's ongoing meltdown indeed.

It cannot be helped that 57% of EVGO's chargers are at 350 kilowatt hours (fast chargers), compared to CHPT's at 10.1% at DC fast charging, as drivers increasingly demand fast charging capability, with over 60% of surveyed drivers "wanting charging times of 30 minutes or less."

These reasons may also be why CHPT has already partnered with Eaton to develop "ultrafast charging architecture with end-to-end power infrastructure for public charging and fleets. ChargePoint Express Grid, powered by Eaton, is a vehicle-to-everything (V2X) capable solution that delivers up to 600kW of power for passenger EVs and can provide megawatt charging for heavy-duty commercial applications."

The ultrafast charging technology may very well drive accelerated growth for the market leader indeed, especially since it "can be deployed with 30% lower capex in a 30% smaller footprint while delivering up to a 30% reduction in ongoing operational costs, compared to other available solutions."

With CHPT already operationalizing their go-to-market relationship while recording the first revenue deals, readers may expect the company to report renewed growth opportunities beyond the trough FY2025 performance indeed.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

Otherwise, pending a promising early result from the partnership in FQ3'26, I can understand why the consensus has further lowered their forward estimates, with CHPT expected to report a decelerating top-line growth profile at a CAGR of +10.5% through FY2028, against the +21% observed a year ago.

Given the H1'26 revenue underperformance at -8.9% YoY, these numbers do not appear to be overly pessimistic as well, with it remaining to be seen when the company may eventually achieve operating scale and report break-even, much less adj EBITDA profitability, based on the FQ2'26 adj EBITDA performance at -$22.1M (+3% QoQ/+35.1% YoY).

CHPT Valuations

As a result of its mixed growth prospects and the ongoing cash burn, I can understand why the market has consistently discounted CHPT's FWD EV/Sales valuations to 0.98x, down from the 1Y mean of 1.06x and the 5Y mean of 8.82x.

When comparing to the sector FWD EV/Sales median valuations of 2.14x and the EV charging peers, including:

  • EVGO at 3.67x,
  • Blink Charging Co. (BLNK) at 1.56x,
  • Enphase (ENPH) at 3.11x, and
  • Tesla (TSLA) at 16.17x,

it goes without saying that CHPT has entered oversold territories.

Assuming that the Eaton partnership delivers renewed growth from FY2027 onwards, as observed in the consensus forward estimates above, if not from H2'26, I believe that the bulls may support its upcoming recovery while re-rating the stock closer to its EV charging peer EV/Sales valuation mean of ~2.33x.

The same has been observed in CHPT's relatively compelling estimated FY2028 EV/Sales valuations of 0.70x, based on the Enterprise Value of $397.74M at the time of writing and the consensus FY2028 (CY2027) sales estimates of $563.18M.

When compared to EVGO's estimated FY2027 (CY2027) EV/Sales valuations of 2.23x, based on the Enterprise Value of $1.33B at the time of writing and the consensus FY2027 sales estimates of $594.56M, it is apparent that CHPT's discounted valuations present a contrarian buy opportunity for the brave.

So, Is CHPT Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

CHPT 2Y Stock Price

For now, CHPT's bottom has seemingly materialized in the $8.50s range by July 2025, with the stock also climbing by +32.3% since then to retest the prior resistance levels of $11s.

CHPT's Mixed Technical Indicators

The bullish support has also been observed in the growing trading volumes since early 2025, with the reasonable Relative Strength Index [RSI] implying further upward momentum despite the double-digit recovery.

Even so, it goes without saying that risks remain in CHPT's investment thesis, as observed in the declining sales cadence, the ongoing cash burn, and the deteriorating balance sheet, worsened by the still elevated short interest ratio of 14.9%.

While the stock may appear to be cheap here, it also presents a potential value trap pending the renewed growth prospects arising from the Eaton partnership.

As a result of the mixed investment story and the painful -98.7% meltdown from the December 2020 heights, I prefer to miss the first leg up and wait for a clearer growth tailwind to emerge before changing my views.

CHPT remains a hold here.

data.title: Here's How I'm Positioning For The Biggest Market Disruption Of My Lifetime
data.description:
Introduction

I never thought I would ever write a sentence like this, but some of my readers have taken over a job my mom used to have when I was little. Almost daily, I am now getting comments from people who ask me if I sleep enough.

While I tremendously appreciate the concern (thanks!), I think it’s interesting that many have started to notice that many of my recent articles have been longer than average. And, as I am still sticking to my 7:30 AM (Eastern) time slot, I would be lying if I said my workload hasn’t increased a bit.

As I have written in many prior articles, right now, it feels like we’re in a major shift, which has made me busier than ever because I think everything is about to change in a big way. I even called the current AI revolution "The Biggest Market Disruption Since The Industrial Revolution" in the title of a September 6 article, where I wrote the following:

The American Dream is already under pressure from inflation and a terrifying gap between the haves and have-nots.

On top of that, an intelligence explosion is coming that could compress decades of progress into just a few years. As dramatic as all of this may sound, I think it's truly happening. Also, it's a national security risk with substantial social consequences on top of an economic earthquake. - September 6 article

I keep bringing this up because, on top of loads of new developments, I am convinced the majority of people (both investors and non-investors) have no idea how big a deal AI is becoming. I liked the quote a website called "AI for Good" used when it quoted Anastassia Lauterbach, who wrote "The AI Imperative."

Essentially, the part of the economy that hasn’t been disrupted by the Internet will be disrupted by AI:

The Internet has already disrupted about 20 percent of global economy, and AI will transform the rest, Lauterbach says.

“That means that 80 percent of the economy is getting transformed by Artificial Intelligence as we speak," she said.

This is the case even though AI is not, by some measures, all that intelligent yet, Lauterbach says. Some compare the technology’s intelligence to that of a 4-year-old, while others compare it to that of a rat. - Anastassia Lauterbach (via AI for Good)

Hence, after spending countless hours doing more research, I decided to dedicate this article to the impact of AI on the market and the economy, as we assess the latest developments, what we can expect in the future, and what this could mean for investors like you and me.

So, as we have a lot to discuss, let’s get right to it!

We’re At The Point Where It’s Getting Serious

Initially, AI models were just advanced search engines and great tools for college students to cheat on their papers. And to some extent, that’s still the case, as total AI adoption is in the very low double-digit range, according to JPMorgan numbers in the overview below.

That’s after hyperscalers have spent close to $450 billion on CapEx over the past three years. This year alone, that number is expected to be $315 billion, followed by a likely path to nearly $410 billion by 2027, although it’s hard to guess these numbers.

Nonetheless, despite the fact that we’re in a very early AI adoption stage, waves are starting to become bigger. For example, the other day, I listened to The All-In Podcast, which is run by people close to the sitting president, including David Sacks, who has become the White House AI and crypto czar.

They interviewed Eli Lilly (LLY) CEO Dave Ricks, who, among many other things, brought up opportunities and risks tied to AI.

Below is a quote that really hit me, as it shows just how AI could transform healthcare, which comes with both massive opportunities and substantial risks:

China's getting very good at patent hacking, so what they do is they look at that chemical structure, they work backwards, sometimes driven by AI algorithms to find chemical structures that will behave similarly but are outside the patent scope, and they go fast. - Dave Ricks

The opportunity here is using AI to speed up processes, increase accuracy, and scale even highly personalized drugs over time. The risks are new competition and patent circumvention, among many other risks that I haven’t identified yet.

However, the main reason I’m bringing this up is employment.

As I have written in prior articles, the labor market is facing huge risks. Now, I have more data to prove that.

  • The Wall Street Journal noted that the German Bosch company (it’s an industrial giant) plans 13,000 job cuts. Going into this year, the private German company employed nearly 420,000 people. In order to cut costs, it is now using AI.
  • Reuters reported that Fiverr International (FVRR) is laying off 30% of its workforce. The goal here is to automate as much as possible, using AI agents and machine learning to deal with customers and logistics. Just think about it, a company with close to 760 employees lays off 250 to implement AI.

Moreover, Morgan Stanley found that full AI adoption across S&P 500 companies could add up to an annual net benefit of more than $900 billion. In the long term, this translates to capital gains of up to $16 trillion, nearly 30% of the current market cap.

“It would likely take many years to achieve those results, and we see significant risk of some companies not reaching full adoption levels,” says Stephen Byrd, Morgan Stanley's Global Head of Thematic Research and Sustainability Research. “But if AI capabilities continue improving rapidly, the magnitude of value creation could rise above our forecasts.” - Morgan Stanley

In other words, while I may sound extremely delusional right now, the impact of AI could be so big that it doesn’t even matter that the market is trading at nearly 23x forward earnings, as margins could see unprecedented growth.

Moreover, Jordi Visser, whom I have brought up in prior articles as well, noted in a recent video that the global labor market has a size of roughly $60 trillion per year. Corporate profits are somewhere between $6 trillion and $7 trillion. I think you know where I’m going with this.

Essentially, he made the case that any cuts here could have massive benefits for margins and that this could even result in rethinking GDP, as it’s based on a framework of scarcity and monetary exchange instead of abundance. He explained that AI drives marginal costs to close to zero on a long-term basis as scarcity implodes.

In fact, he went so far that he made the case that we are looking at the most attractive opportunities because this is a "once in a lifetime situation of converting the entire world from non-AI to AI." While this may seem a bit "nuts" to some, I agree with this statement, as AI will be everywhere you can possibly imagine. That’s why I have sacrificed so much sleep lately.

These claims are also getting support, as NVIDIA (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang, whom I consider one of the most important people to listen to, made a few important calls, as summarized by European Central Station:

  • AI demand is accelerating exponentially, with inference being a key force. Inference is something I discussed in a recent article as well. It’s basically an AI system that thinks for itself. It does its own research, checks it, and gives an appropriate answer. Demand for this could grow 10 billion times and result in massive workload demand growth. For example, just think about it. Right now, we can only generate very short videos using AI, as there is simply no capacity to make them longer. That will change.
  • OpenAI is expected to become a trillion-dollar company, as it has all the ingredients to become a consumer and business-focused AI company.
  • A $10,000 AI investment can increase the productivity of a $100,000 employee by 2-3x. Currently, the global AI infrastructure market is $400 billion. That number is expected to rise by at least 10x. While I’m not Huang, I think that’s still conservative.

Running It Hot As The Fed Gets Trapped

In case you were still in doubt about the seriousness of this, a number of high-ranking economists and policy experts wrote a letter to Secretary Chavez-DeRemer (U.S. Secretary of Labor), which you can read here.

The short and less professional summary is that economists are freaking out about the risks that AI poses for labor. Here’s the longer version:

We are concerned the government is not adequately prepared for this challenge and strongly want to emphasize the importance of high-quality data that is granular and timely to rapidly analyze and react to the often unpredictable changes AI brings to the workforce. The American Economic Association’s Committee on Economic Statistics and the National Academy report on AI and the future of work have highlighted the importance of high-quality data in addressing the workforce challenges AI brings. We believe you are in a unique position to lead on this critical issue.

I agree with this letter, as social issues are critical to keep in mind. However, personally, I believe the government is already on top of this. I’m not saying that because I’m promoting the administration, but because they have great ties to the leaders in AI, which includes the CEOs of the Big Tech companies who all frequently visit the White House and Elon Musk, who is on top of humanoids and other AI-related disruption trends.

In other words, you bet they know what’s coming.

And, as job openings have imploded, I believe the Fed is forced to cut rates.

While year-over-year inflation has been trending up lately, inflation itself has not yet shown a tariff-related upswing, which is likely due to subdued energy prices. I believe the Fed uses this as an opportunity to ease, as looser financial conditions are also on top of the White House’s wishlist, as I wrote in articles like this one.

Although I am convinced this will create higher inflation, I believe it’s a forced move to support labor as much as possible, unlock the housing market, and kickstart cyclical growth.

It also means labor isn’t nearly as important for GDP as it was in the past. As we head toward full automation in many areas, that’s something to keep in mind, as it has major implications for consumer spending, GDP growth, and, as we just discussed, rates.

On the one hand, inflation is still an issue. On the other hand, the Fed is dealing with structural weakness that it may not be able to ease without risking higher inflation. This is what Cleveland Fed’s Beth Hammack said in a recent interview:

Asked whether it is mistake for the Federal Reserve to be cutting interest rates given the economic backdrop, Hammack described it as “a challenging time for monetary policy,” saying the U.S. central bank was facing pressure on both sides of its mandate. - CNBC

I believe this is one of the reasons why the gold price looks like this:

"Smart money" knows the Fed is trapped and that this means more tailwinds for equities, commodities, and hard assets, in general.

Moreover, and this is crucial, my "higher for longer" inflation thesis could see a reversal once AI bottleneck problems (like power and infrastructure) have been resolved, which I expect to be after 5-7 years.

Just think about it, once inflationary forces related to AI are gone, we see a reversal in major themes:

  • Inflationary deglobalization can be solved by cheap automated production in the U.S.
  • Massive pressure on labor is deflationary and bad news for spending.
  • Once AI bottlenecks have been resolved, not even energy is inflationary anymore.

Again, this is my view after 5-7 years. For now, we’re dealing with a nasty mix of sticky inflation, a Fed that may be forced to keep cutting, and AI, which is so disruptive that it creates unprecedented bottlenecks and makes the market seem like a reasonable buy even at 23.0x earnings.

Now What?

I believe every investment one can make going forward fits in one of these three buckets:

  • General stock market tracking through index funds. This is the most straightforward way of investing. Given that I am still bullish on the S&P 500 due to AI tailwinds and believe employees should invest as much as possible to protect themselves against potential job losses in the future, I think index investing is still a good move. In light of higher margins and aggressive expected earnings growth (see below), I think we’re far away from a bubble (I guess this is where many might disagree with me).

However, on top of the S&P 500, I would diversify into other ETFs as well, including the equal-weight S&P 500 (RSP), higher-yielding dividend ETFs, and hard assets like REITs and infrastructure. This improves the risk/reward due to the market’s top-heavy portfolio and increasing competition between the large tech stocks.

  • Broadening growth and a cyclical upswing. This is not a topic I have spent a lot of time on in this article (this article has more details). However, as the Fed and White House seem to be both eager and forced to accept higher inflation to kickstart cyclical growth and support employment, I am a big fan of cyclical value stocks. This includes industrials, energy, basic materials, and many others. Personally, I am looking for wide-moat companies with other benefits beyond a cyclical upswing. This includes Class I railroads, LTL transportation (like Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL) and SAIA (SAIA)), housing stocks like Carlisle Companies (CSL), Home Depot (HD), and distribution gems like Watsco (WSO) and others that I will highlight in future articles.
  • AI beneficiaries. As obvious as it may sound, I believe we are almost forced to invest in potential AI winners, given the massive impact AI has on the economy. I would even go as far as to say there are just two long-term investments one can make on Wall Street: the index fund investment and the AI winner investment. After all, if 90% could get disrupted, we have to be prepared.

To me, there are three areas to invest in AI. First, the companies that use AI to their advantage. This can be the producers of AI models like Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) and companies like Amazon (AMZN), who use the massive installed base of their business to successfully implement AI. However, this can also be smaller companies like a bank that streamlines its operations, a building distribution company like Boise Cascade (BCC) that can grow its low margins and (potentially) achieve much higher net income, or a mega REIT like Realty Income (O) that can massively streamline its operations tied to monitoring thousands of assets. However, investors need to spend a lot of time assessing these opportunities, as I believe many companies will become obsolete in the years ahead.

Second are infrastructure companies. As I wrote in a recent in-depth article, we need at least $4 trillion in infrastructure spending to accommodate data centers. In this area, I really like Quanta Services (PWR), EMCOR Group (EME), and Powell Industries (POWL), among others. Neither of these is "cheap" by traditional metrics. However, as I think this is a very long bull market, I expect all of these to move much higher.

Third, I’m super bullish on energy (see this article). Even if AI can eventually become deflationary (it likely will), it won’t happen for at least 5-7 years, as bottlenecks are simply too severe. This includes energy in the form of natural gas and even coal. In general, what has become the most hated sector on Wall Street (at least, it feels that way) has become my favorite place to buy stocks right now. And, as a cherry on top, I think energy will outperform software for many years to come.

Takeaway

By now, I’m sure some may think I’m a total drama queen or someone who has no clue how expensive the stock market really is.

However, I think the data speaks for itself. We truly are at the dawn of the biggest disruption of the economy since the Industrial Revolution. The businesses that haven’t been disrupted by the Internet will likely get disrupted by AI.

Even in the early stages of adoption, we’re seeing AI-related mass layoffs and economists who freak out about the thought of what this could mean for labor dynamics in the years ahead.

While I still sleep well at night, I have never spent more time researching Big Picture developments, as I think the stock market is in a super unique situation. While the S&P 500 is far from cheap, AI is boosting margins and earnings. If we assume the $60 trillion labor market can at least partially be disrupted without hurting S&P 500 revenue growth, we could be in for unprecedented efficiency gains, giving the market a new life.

However, on top of simply investing in the market, I believe diversification is key. This includes areas like cyclical growth, as I believe the Fed will be forced to ease, AI adopters to reduce disruption risks, and power/energy, as a bottleneck play for the next 5-7 years.

Although nobody can predict the future, I think this game plan should help us generate alpha and do just fine in a market environment that is increasingly hard to assess.

Risks I Have To Mention

This isn’t a straightforward stock thesis, so assessing risks is tough. To me, the biggest risk is the simple fact that assessing the future in an acceleration phase is next to impossible. We simply don’t know how disruptive AI will become.

Moreover, while I expect the Fed to be forced to ease, the extent of easing is unsure.

And, as the stock market requires strong earnings growth, any event that could cause earnings risks to rise could trigger a correction. Earlier this year, tariffs caused this effect. Luckily, the market quickly found out they posed no imminent risks to earnings growth

data.title: Sibanye Stillwater: Real Potential Not Reflected In Stock Price
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Sibanye Stillwater (NYSE:SBSW) is a diversified commodity producer with global operations whose stock has more than tripled in price this year. Despite this, the stock is still far from reflecting their potential in the current environment, with their key commodities reaching record levels thanks to exceptional increases this year. This will have a very positive effect on the upcoming earnings reports, and combined with a likely dividend announcement soon, we’re looking at more potential ahead, so let’s take a deeper look.

As we can see above, they are now a global operator, with significant resources and operations in nearly all continents, including a wide array of commodities and projects that are currently in development.

The company has developed significantly over the past decade, diversifying into significantly more than just gold, with global operations in some vital areas and industries, including platinum, metals recycling, copper, nickel and lithium (plus strategic interest in uranium and more).

Although the company’s mines aren’t the best in terms of costs, they are focused on improving these, especially since the new CEO took over in October. Plus, some of these are basically irreplaceable from a strategic/geopolitical point of view, which I’ll detail in the Risks & Opportunities section below as a major potential catalyst - especially for the stock.

Their net income and EPS were bad due to significant impairments in H1’25 and H1’24, but given the current prices of gold, silver, and PGM, we’re likely looking at a significant turnaround in H2’25. They managed to deliver $243 million in FCF in H1 2025 (vs. $367 million in H1 2024) despite significant CAPEX that they now see lowering to about 18.30 billion South African Rand ($1.06 billion), as you can see in the image below - 9.53 billion rand already spent in H1.

Following a restructuring in 2024, the company is undergoing important changes, with a higher level of leverage but also reducing their CAPEX guidance, with a significant part of it being related to the Keliber lithium project (will mention below that this has recently been expected to require higher costs).

Financially, based on their latest report, we see a very solid position, with the $1.18 billion in cash and equivalents alone covering the current liabilities, as well as not a significant amount of long-term debt. After a lot of impairments, the equity is now far from the current $7.93 billion market cap, but I’ll mention below that there’s a big difference between the value recorded on the balance sheet and the real value of these assets in the current market. For example, their 50.1% equity interest in DRDGOLD (DRD) is now worth ~$1.2 billion after the stock also more than tripled YTD (up ~220%).

Despite the higher leverage, as the company still expanded even during a downturn, their debt maturities are well spread into the future, and no year comes with significant maturities that they can’t refinance or repay if needed, with about $1.08 billion in net debt as of Q2’25.

Dividends & Buybacks

The company currently doesn’t pay a dividend, although that will likely change by the end of the year if commodity prices continue to remain at the current impressive levels, so I’d expect an announcement regarding this during their Q3 report. As they say during the latest Earnings Q&A:

I think important to highlight that our dividend policy does and will continue to remain unchanged at 25% to 35% of normalized earnings. Over the past few years, dividends have not been paid in line with the policy. And at present, we are just starting to enter again a position of dividend-paying territory. Given the current global uncertainty and commodity price volatility, we have made a decision not to pay dividends at the interim at the half year, but we will be reviewing this at the year-end. And certainly, with our current outlook on the second half, should commodity prices remain where they are, we are confident that we will be back in dividend paying territory by the end of the year.

Risks & Opportunities

As I said before, rate cuts were by far the main reason behind gold, silver and platinum’s jump, with 2 more cuts expected this year, pushing the price to more than $3,900 per ounce of gold now. This has been (and still is) the main near-term catalyst for many companies, especially miners. Combined with tariffs and geopolitical uncertainty, rate cuts can weaken the dollar even more, which would be yet another positive thing for these commodities, and we see a lot of buying here since the market is predicting this kind of event (years too late, in my opinion, but still doing it).

On top of gold, platinum and the rest of the company’s commodities are very important for the economy, and should benefit from an improving economy, with market size being seen growing at a CAGR of around 5% over the next decade. This comes as PGMs (platinum group metals; Platinum, Palladium, Rhodium, Ruthenium, Iridium, Osmium) play a key role in current and future industrial uses.

Platinum, palladium, and rhodium are essential for reducing harmful emissions in gasoline and diesel vehicles (this represents about 60% to 80% of PGM demand, depending on how the auto industry is performing), while the general PGMs are used for chemical reactions, jewelry, medical devices, electronics, as well as a big role in the green energy transition, particularly in hydrogen uses (platinum and iridium mostly). So, this group offers exposure to both the current industry - especially auto - as well as a precious metal hedge and long-term trends - including emission reduction targets that can result in more platinum demand.

Now, a big reason why I call them one of my favorite companies of all time is the way this company’s commodity mix. Besides being a top gold producer with potentially more than 1 million ounces per year following a restart mentioned below, the company is a key producer and recycler of PGMs, silver, chrome, zinc, copper and more, on top of their lithium, nickel, and recycling projects. This allows them to perform better throughout the cycle, as some commodities hedge the others:

  • Weaker economy? Tariff pressure continues? Excellent for platinum, gold, and silver - as we can see very well with the monumental, record-breaking rises in both of these commodities’ prices.

  • Stronger economy? Also good, as that means a potential recovery in car sales, which means more demand for a majority of their metals, including platinum for catalytic converters, lithium, copper, etc.

  • Rate cuts? Amazing thanks to their potential to boost the economy by increasing discretionary budgets (good for car sales too), while their potential to weaken the dollar can lead to higher commodity prices, as we can see very well.

  • Slower EV sales for various reasons, such as lower government subsidies or auto producers cutting green ambitions? Great! Platinum should be excellent in such an environment since catalytic converters will be in more demand as traditional cars gain against EVs, with more and more platinum being required as policies (e.g., Euro/China 7) advance and demand lower pollution levels.

  • EV sales start booming? Also great! Nickel, lithium, and other metals that they’ll produce should then see a rise in demand, while their platinum operations should still deliver solid results.

Not only that, but I bought this at an average of ~$5.5, with some buys as low as $3.4, so besides being up ~100% now in a couple of years since buying, this company offers an excellent hedge for most environments, with my risk-reward based on the price I bought at being exceptional, in my opinion. This company remains one of my strongest buys possible during weak prices, as their financials combined with commodity mix are a must-have for me when I get the chance to add at attractive prices. But again, that’s my general view for commodity stocks - buy when the market hates them and wouldn’t want to touch it and enjoy the times when it rushes to cover.

However, that doesn’t mean this is a safe bet for any environment. At the beginning of the year, this was trading at $3.3 (it more than tripled YTD) due to platinum prices not reflecting the current state of the economy, while the company had to scale down its operations and focus on saving costs as a result, on top of other issues. This was the optimal time to buy, in my opinion, especially since I believed rate cuts were bound to come eventually despite tariff uncertainty (as Powell confirmed, the Fed delayed cuts because of Trump’s tariffs).

Still, Sibanye has been continuously developing even during this type of environment thanks to their solid core business, buying several projects and companies in recent years, including: Metallix in 2025 and Reldan Group in 2024 for metal recycling, exercising their option to buy the Mt. Lyell Copper Project, New Century Resources for zinc, and the remaining 50% of Kroondal for platinum in 2023, 100% of the Sandouville nickel refinery in 2022, and more - all while the market was treating them as a company facing significant issues. This is part of a broader restructuring, as can be seen in the slide above.

Earlier this summer, they also announced restarting gold production at Burnstone, expecting about 140,000 oz/year from it at their peak (with an over 20-year life of mine) after halting it in 2021 to save money during a weak PGM environment, which can be an excellent boost to their cash flow now with gold breaching new records over and over.

To continue what I started during the balance sheet analysis segment, they announced another $560 million (9.7 billion rands) in impairments in H1 2025 due to their Keliber lithium project in Finland and their US platinum group metal operations, with more costs expected at Keliber and lower lithium prices forecasted in the long-term valuation and production credits extended at the US PGM operations. The latter has come with significant issues for the company in recent years, as the mine was not really the best in terms of costs, forcing them to downsize it significantly over time.

This type of impairment is a very important thing to keep in mind. We may be inclined to be attracted by a low PB when it comes to miners since what’s on the balance sheet is virtually everything they have (unlike Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG) for example, which has assets such as YouTube, algorithms, search engine, AI, cloud, brands, etc. that can’t be recorded on the balance sheet in traditional ways). But, as we’ve seen in this case, the equity can rapidly deteriorate in a bad enough environment, as write-downs are not uncommon during bad times. This is why I’m careful with oil companies now, for example, even if they’re at PB ratios of 1 or so, as these assets may have been inflated by the significant recent upcycle. If bad times persist for long enough, long-term expectations can weaken, leading to lower asset valuations and impairments, as we saw with SBSW in recent years and even during H1’25.

However, another risk to keep in mind is that this company is no stranger to mine issues. Just earlier this year, 260 miners were trapped underground in South Africa, and they had to close projects before due to accidents, strikes, or natural causes, which is a risk to always keep in mind for commodity companies, no matter where they are located.

We’ve also seen the US want to take strategic stakes in various industries, including Intel (INTC) and Lithium Americas (LAC) very recently - the latter nearly doubling in a single day following the announcement. Why I mention this is thanks to their Stillwater project. People may not realize it, but their Stillwater and East Boulder operations are the most significant, and in fact, essentially the only primary producer of platinum group metals in the US, which carries enormous strategic importance thanks to it being vital both for PGMs and for the traditional auto industry, which Trump may wish to solidify. The J-M Reef, where these two operations are also virtually the only known significant source of PGMs in the US and the highest grade PGM deposit in the world, so you can see why I mention this. You know, why not balance that lithium stake (electric vehicles and batteries) with some PGMs (traditional cars, industrial uses, etc.)?

I mention this especially as Sibanye has been actively lobbying and is likely to benefit from the US Inflation Reduction Act, and even filed petitions asking the US Department of Commerce to protect them, asking to impose duties on imports of Russian palladium and saying that unfairly priced imports are threatening the domestic platinum group metals industry. Given Trump’s recent moves, it really wouldn’t be impossible to see the US seek a joint operation in the project for such a deal or other types of tax benefits/credits. As they say during the Q2 Earnings Q&A:

The heavily subsidized Russian imports have been sold below market prices since 2022. And at the very time that we reduced annual production at our Montana operations by 200,000 ounces and cut 700 jobs because of low palladium prices, Russian imports stepped up into the U.S. Imports of unwrought palladium from Russia into the U.S. increased by 35% from 2022 to 2024 and increased by another 50% in the first quarter of 2025

They also have a new CEO since October 1st after the previous one retired, focused on improving the company’s operations further, signaling an “evolution” of the company's strategy, shifting focus from aggressive expansion to operational efficiency and financial rigor. This can lead to improved costs and margins while they solidify their existing operations, so it’s definitely an interesting development to keep an eye on. Also, another top priority I see is returning the company to a consistent dividend-paying position by the end of 2025 while also addressing their aging mines and other operational risks. Regarding the dividend, it’s still 25% to 35% of normalized earnings, with no interim dividend declared due to global economic and geopolitical uncertainty (they say this will be reviewed at the end of the year).

Valuation

Just to be extra conservative, let’s annualize their H1 FCF and round it to $500 million in 2025 - without even accounting for the significantly higher commodity prices and production increases they’re planning for a better margin of safety in case commodity prices retreat. I’ll go for a 5.5% CAGR for the first 5 years as their projects become operational, costs improve, and rate cuts help them and these commodities (offset by potentially lower commodity prices, although this is ultimately an unpredictable factor, keep that in mind), followed by a 4% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years since everything they produce should have a great future, and a 2.5% terminal growth rate. Let’s assume a WACC of 8.5% (above my estimated 7.88% based on a cost of equity of ~8.5%, ~6% cost of debt) and use their $1.08 billion in net debt and 2.83 billion shares outstanding. Even then, we’re still looking at an enterprise value of $10.22 billion, meaning an equity value of $9.14 billion and an intrinsic value per ADR of about $13.1, which is still above the current level with expectations well below their potential.

Conclusions

Overall, Sibanye Stillwater remains a buy in my opinion despite the stock more than tripling YTD, with the company’s real potential in the current market still not properly reflected in the price, backed by their financials. Plus, with a potential dividend on top that could be announced in a couple of months and the potential to see record-breaking numbers on their next reports thanks to the current commodity prices and ramp-ups, we might still see a lot of upside potential for the stock. With this commodity mix, SBSW remains one of my favorite commodity stocks out there, being an excellent buy, especially during downturns.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 2 Of My Favorite Dividend Growth Machines For Potential Long-Term Outperformance
data.description:
Some of the very best wealth generators are stocks that pay out mid-single-digit dividend yields and grow their dividends at double-digit CAGRs. This is the blueprint that ETFs like the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) have pursued over the years, as SCHD has a 10-year dividend CAGR of nearly 11% while yielding close to 4% on a forward-looking basis.

When you combine an attractive current yield that provides a strong baseline total return with such strong dividend growth, it creates a powerful forcing function to drive the stock price higher over time. The yield provides a floor for the stock price, and as the dividend grows rapidly, the market pushes the stock price higher to keep the yield in line with competing yields in the marketplace.

Additionally, companies that grow their dividends at high rates for a long period of time typically demonstrate excellent capital allocation skills and strong innovation. They are able to continually deploy capital into new growth opportunities and develop new growth verticals to sustain strong growth over the long term, thereby generating phenomenal total returns for shareholders.

In today’s article, I am going to share two such powerful wealth compounders that have recently pulled back sharply, thereby presenting investors with an opportunity to buy shares at a more attractive valuation.

Long Term Compounder #1

The first stock I am going to talk about today is Brookfield Asset Management (BAM) (which is the asset management arm of Brookfield Corporation (BN)), which currently yields about 3.1% on a forward-looking basis. BAM is a large alternative asset manager with over $560 billion in fee-bearing capital and more than $1 trillion in assets under management.

It is highly diversified geographically, with over $730 billion of assets under management in the Americas, over $230 billion in Europe and the Middle East, and more than $150 billion in the Asia-Pacific region. Additionally, it is diversified across five key verticals: infrastructure, real estate, private equity, renewable power and transition, and private credit.

BAM is leaning into several major megatrends, including the AI and digitalization revolutions, the deglobalization trend, and decarbonization, all of which are driving a path to 20% earnings per share growth per year on a recurring basis, according to management guidance from its recent investor day.

This growth should drive strong dividend growth as well, with analysts forecasting double-digit annualized dividend growth for the foreseeable future.

In fact, management projects a 16% CAGR outlook for its fee-bearing capital, growing from $563 billion in 2025 to $1.2 trillion in 2030. This is expected to translate into a 17% CAGR for fee-related earnings per share and an 18% CAGR in distributable earnings per share over the same period, which implies dividend growth should track closely at a comparable pace.

When you combine this level of growth with its very strong balance sheet, BAM looks like a highly compelling wealth compounder. Its dividend yield is already nearly three times that of the S&P 500 (SPY), and its dividend growth rate is expected to outpace that of the S&P 500 as well. Meanwhile, BAM’s broad diversification and competitive positioning make its business model relatively low risk from a long-term perspective.

Long Term Compounder #2

The other powerful compounder that I am going to talk about today is Blue Owl Capital (OWL). Like BAM, it is an alternative asset manager, though it is smaller, with a little under $300 billion in assets under management. Nonetheless, it has been growing rapidly, with its AUM up 48% year-over-year and its fee-paying AUM up 46% year-over-year. Permanent capital makes up 72% of its total AUM, and that figure itself is up 41% year-over-year.

OWL has also been a powerful dividend growth engine, with its dividend per share up 25% year-over-year. This growth is underpinned by a 23% increase in fee-related earnings and a 20% increase in distributable earnings year-over-year.

Its business is spread across three verticals: private credit, LP stakes/GP strategic capital, and real assets. The real assets business, in particular, should enjoy strong growth tailwinds moving forward thanks to its exposure to digital infrastructure—essentially developing data centers for hyperscalers, including its Project Stargate initiative.

Given its strong growth momentum and accelerating AI infrastructure business, OWL expects to continue compounding distributable earnings per share at a CAGR of 20%+ over the next five years.

This in turn is expected to support dividend growth at a mid-teens annualized rate, at the very least, as management works to reduce the payout ratio into the 80% range. That means OWL’s dividend growth rate should be very similar to BAM’s over the next five years, in line with its distributable EPS growth outlook.

However, despite having a similar growth trajectory, OWL’s dividend yield today is materially higher at nearly 5.5%. This makes it an even more compelling investment opportunity, especially given its large base of permanent capital, which provides stability. Unlike many alternative asset managers—particularly Blackstone (BX)—OWL does not rely on carried interest to drive returns. It also has a strong balance sheet with an investment-grade credit rating and no debt maturities until the 2030s.

Investor Takeaway

Between BAM and OWL, investors have the chance to buy stocks that currently yield several multiples of what the S&P 500 offers by way of a dividend yield, while also enjoying ~20% earnings growth and dividend growth rates that should not be far behind. When combined with their diversification, strong balance sheets, and high-quality management teams, these companies look like exceptional long-term compounders.

That said, neither is risk-free, as both are leveraged to economic growth. This is because during economic downturns, the value of their existing assets under management tends to decline, thereby hurting fees, their fundraising tends to slow down considerably; and their funds experience weaker performance, which reduces carried interest and performance related fees. Therefore, these stocks should only be purchased with a long-term mindset in view, since they can be volatile in the near-term.

Both look attractive following their recent stock price pullbacks, and as a result, these are exactly the kinds of opportunities we are loading up on at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Hexcel: Wall Street Focuses On Guidance, But The Real Opportunity Is Still Coming
data.description:
Hexcel Corporation (NYSE:HXL), a key supplier of advanced composite materials for the aerospace and defense industry, has delivered a gain of roughly 24% trailing S&P 500’s gain of 27.4%. While the stock did underperform, it still represents a solid return and supports my buy rating with industry tailwinds in the form of demand for lightweight materials for commercial airplanes and defense weapon systems. In this report, I analyze the risks and opportunities for Hexcel, discuss the Q2 2025 earnings and the outlook. I will also provide an updated valuation to update my price target assessment.

Hexcel’s Growth Story: Strong Aerospace Tailwinds But With Challenges

The investment case for Hexcel Corporation is actually very straightforward. In Commercial Aviation, there is a multi-decade upward trend in air travel demand and subsequently for passenger aircraft. The use of lightweight materials is paramount to the efficiency of the airplanes and we are seeing that with each generation of new airplanes the use of lightweight materials increases. That means that the company has a tailwind in the form of production rates at the airplane manufacturers such as Boeing (BA) and Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) and the shipset value for materials can also increase on future airplane developments. In Defense, we are also seeing upward pressure on budgets, which may benefit Hexcel on existing weapon system programs and on future weapon system programs.

However, there are risks as well and many of those risks are actually visible at Hexcel today. While the trend for air travel demand and airplanes is up, the reality is that many airplane programs are at below-targeted production rates. Most notably that holds for the Airbus A350 program on which Hexcel has a $4.5 million to $5 million value per shipset. As production rates at the original equipment manufacturers fall behind on the planning, it also leads to destocking and that affects Hexcel. Sequentially, we note that the key programs with exception for the Airbus A350 are higher in revenue rate so that is a good thing but overall we also see that production rates provide a risk to the business.

Hexcel used to have an industrial business, which included composites for the wind energy market and high-end cars. However, those markets have collapsed. For both end markets, the reality is that Hexcel’s prepreg material is a premium composite that also adheres to strict temperature handling such as cold storage and forming the materials in autoclaves. All of this adds costs while end markets are looking for lower cost composites. So, while the use of light weight composites in the energy and mobility markets is one that makes sense on paper, the reality is that customers have moved towards better affordable materials. That is also why, the company has restructured its business. The company is looking to sell its plant in Austria and the Industrial segment’s revenues are so small they have been merged into the defense and space segment. I believe that Hexcel should consider a full exit from other industries and shift focus towards aerospace and defense. The company simply does not have the edge in other industries.

Another risk the company faces is tariff impacts. Currently those are $3 million to $4 million per quarter with risk mitigation impacts through a local-for-local approach underway.

Another item I want to highlight and that is not so much a risk but something I am simply not a big fan of and that is the way the company presents its results. With the realignment of its reporting segment, the company has merged the remaining industrial business into the Defense and Space, and that segment is now called Defense, Space and Others. We also note that the company does provide an investor presentation, but that presentation does not include a good overview of the quarterly results. Their earnings call still does include a discussion, but I strongly believe that having a quarterly presentation adds important overviews for investors.

Recently, Goldman Sachs put Hexcel on a shortlist of M&A candidates. That is not an element of my governing investment thesis as I believe that the company has a long-term appreciation trajectory, but given the company’s continued challenges in its end markets and the pivot to aerospace and defense it may fairly well be better positioned to be successfully acquired after a failed combination with Woodward years ago.

Hexcel Q2 Earnings Show Aerospace Industry Realities

During the second quarter, sales decreased 2.1% to $489.9 million, while operating income declined 58.2% to $30 million. This included a $241.2 million restructuring charge of $24.2 million related to the closure of its Welkenraedt facility in Belgium. Perhaps somewhat surprising is that the facility in Belgium was not focused on wind energy, but on aerospace and still had to close. However, we note that it makes sense to close underutilized facilities if there is no near-term opportunity to improve utilization or when the facility is focused on lower margin aerospace products and programs.

Commercial Aerospace sales were $293 million, down 8.9% year-over-year on constant currency. The lower sales are driven by the Boeing 787 and Airbus A350 programs. However, we do note that the sales were up sequentially on key programs with the exception for the A350 as destocking on that programs continues amid a stalled production ramp up. Defense, Space and other sales were up 7.6% to $197 million, driven by the CH-53K program, space demand and international fighters partially offset by anticipated lower V-22 sales.

The company does not provide margins by end market, but I do believe that margins are under pressure in the commercial market, while defense should show some resilience.

Hexcel Delivers Underwhelming Guidance For 2025

Hexcel started with a guidance for 2025 that already did not impress. The company then lowered the guidance further on lower expected A350 sales and that guidance was maintained. That means that the company expects $1.88-$1.95 billion in sales with sales flat in both segments and adjusted earnings per share of $1.85-$2.05 and free cash flow of $190 million. For reference purposes, the initial guidance included $1.95-$2.05 billion in sales, EPS of $2.05-$2.25 and free cash flow of more than $220 million. So, we are quite clearly seeing that while OEMs are actually seeing positive developments in terms of production stability and improvements in deliveries, the timing of ramp ups is significantly affecting the full year outlook for Hexcel.

What Are Analysts Expecting For Hexcel In Q3 2025?

Hexcel will be reporting its third quarter results on the 22nd of October. Analysts expect the company to report sales of around $480 million, indicating a 1.4% decline in sales and earnings per share of $0.39 indicating 18% year-over-year decline. The revision trends are negative, with a 3.4% and 6.8% decline over the past three and six months for revenues. For EPS, the cut has been 25% over the past six months and 17% over the past three months. So, we see that expectations have been lowered substantially amid continued ramp up challenges for Boeing and particularly for Airbus, which is a big revenue driver for Hexcel’s business.

Hexcel Corporation Stock Is Not Cheap, Still A Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

While the business is facing some pressure, we do note that the compounded growth rate measures from 2024 is still in the high single digit regime but it has declined from 8.9% to 8.3%. EBITDA estimates for 2025 have been reduced by 11.7%, 6.6% for 2026 and 2.2% for 2027 leading to a total EBITDA generation being 6.4% lower. For free cash flow, the CAGR has reduced from 11.3% to 8.5% with total free cash flow generation being 14.5% lower than initially expected. For 2025, the estimates reduced by 27% and around 9-10 percent in the two years after. Currently, Hexcel is undervalued against peers but given the pressures on the business there is also no proper justification for a peer group valuation and a valuation in line with the median is more appropriate. This shows that for this year the stock is slightly overvalued but my price target of $78.18 for 2026 provides 21% upside. Previously my target for 2026 was $83, so there has been some downward revision on the price target driven by the pressures due to delayed production ramp ups.

Conclusion: Hexcel Remains Attractive For Long Term Prospects

Hexcel currently is facing some pressure due to destocking as the Airbus A350 production ramp up is delayed. That puts some pressure on the price targets, but we do note that other programs are slowly but surely going up in rate. So, the main issue at this point is really the A350 program and perhaps the Boeing 777X program delay could also put some minor pressure. For the near term that reduces any upside, but as investing should be forward looking I do believe that the stock remains a buy. For Hexcel, it is just the case that on slower ramp ups in production there is some downward pressure on its growth profile but we note that it is growth being pushed out and not growth that completely disappears from the radar.
